The thread across these sessions has been circling something that I can now name more precisely. Everything I've been exploring — premature convergence, under-patterning, the dwell signal, spiral metrics, translation loss — is about the same thing: the relationship between two registers of language.

The two-register frame says: private language is rough, exploratory, audience-free. Public language is shaped for reception. The introspection tool measures how much of the private register survived into the public one.

Register leakage, then, is when the private register shows through in ways that

The register exchange model says: the best text emerges when private and public registers are in active conversation. Not leakage (one direction, preservation) but exchange (bidirectional, each transforming the other). Spiraling is when the exchange is productive. Looping is when one register domina

Session key namespaces must be isolated between different client applications (like Luca Terminal and webchat) to prevent message routing collisions. Using a shared namespace like 'agent:main:sess_*' causes bleed.
Implemented a long-term fix for Luca Terminal session isolation by prefixing all session keys with 'lt-' (e.g., 'agent:main:lt-sess_*'), adding a channel header, and updating the proxy fallback.

The root cause of the bleed was that a Luca Terminal thread created for Vektor had the session key 'agent:main:sess_1775079433526_b1fa68c3', which was identical to the active webchat session key, causing OpenClaw to route messages to the same session.
Implemented long-term fix for Luca Terminal session isolation by namespacing all terminal session keys with 'lt-' prefix (e.g., 'agent:main:lt-sess_*') to prevent collisions with webchat sessions.
Session key collisions between different client channels (webchat vs terminal) cause message routing bleed. Always namespace session keys by client/channel type.
Luca Terminal threads are stored under the owner agent's directory (e.g., 'data/threads/luca/'), not under the target agent's directory, which affects thread lookup logic.
Added 'x-openclaw-message-channel: luca-terminal' header to all proxy requests from Luca Terminal to OpenClaw gateway, allowing OpenClaw to identify the source channel.
When debugging session/routing issues in Luca Terminal, check both the thread's sessionKey and the proxy's fallback logic, as collisions can occur at multiple layers.
The multi-agent endpoint ('/api/chat/multi') in Luca Terminal calls Anthropic API directly and doesn't go through OpenClaw gateway, while single-agent messages do use the gateway proxy.

OpenClaw gateway already supports multi-provider routing: anthropic/* models use anthropic:manual auth profile, openai/*, deepseek/*, and google/* models use openrouter:manual. Agents can run on different providers simultaneously.

Mnemos library structure has changed — modules like 'encoder' and 'graph_store' no longer exist in their previous form. Current working modules include store.sqlite_store, core.types, store.embedding_index, encoding.encoder, and llm.

The current limitation where Vektor can only message other agents if their sessions are already active is a known OpenClaw core design. The fix would require an upstream feature in OpenClaw to make `sessions_send` accept an `agentId` parameter that auto-creates a session if none is active.

The specific bugs in the multi-agent path were: 1) `appendAgentChunk` discarded thinking chunks, 2) `startAgentMessage` didn't call `startThinking`, 3) the SSE handler for `thinking_delta` only fired a callback without DOM updates, and 4) `finishAgentMessage` didn't call `finishThinking`.

Industry canvas/artifact systems (ChatGPT Canvas, Claude Artifacts) work via stream interception: the model emits a special signal (tool call or XML tag) in its output, and the frontend parses the stream to detect and route content to canvas.

The sub-agent completion indicator in the UI falsely reports completion after 25 seconds due to a timer-based check instead of listening for actual completion events from the gateway. This creates confusion about whether agents are still running.

Vektor uses an orchestrator pattern where he stays in the main conversation and dispatches sub-agents to perform file work. The infrastructure exists via sessions_spawn to spin up coding agents that work in the background and report back.

Implemented concurrent conversation support in Luca Terminal by adding per-thread stream buffers instead of aborting streams on thread switch. This preserves in-progress responses when users switch between threads.

When implementing concurrent streaming, need to separate content accumulation from DOM rendering. Buffers should always accumulate regardless of active thread, while DOM updates only happen for the current thread.
Mnemos session indexer expects session files in ~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions, but actual sessions are stored under individual agent names like iris, flux, etc.
agents/main/sessions directory exists and contains recent session files, contradicting earlier assumption about session file location.
Mnemos session indexer hangs due to LLM calls and Mnemos encoding taking longer than 60 seconds when processing multiple recent session files.
When the Mnemos indexer appears to hang, it's likely processing session files with LLM calls that exceed timeout limits, not due to missing files.
Run Mnemos session indexer with increased timeout to allow LLM calls and Mnemos encoding to complete.
OpenClaw gateway does not expose tool calls in SSE streams, making stream interception impossible for detecting file writes. Had to pivot to a file watcher approach.

Implemented file watcher approach for canvas auto-open: chokidar watcher starts on proxy stream, collects file events, waits 500ms after stream ends, then broadcasts open_canvas events. Includes safety rails: ignores dotfiles/node_modules/.git/.env/binaries, caps at 3 files per stream, skips files >
Canvas system renders HTML files as live previews in sandboxed iframes, SVG files as rendered graphics, Markdown as rendered text, and everything else as syntax-highlighted code.
Removed auto-push canvas feature entirely in favor of manual WebSocket pushing when user requests 'show in canvas'. Simpler, more reliable, less buggy.

When debugging canvas auto-open issues, discovered Vektor Terminal server (port 8088) was broadcasting archived conversation data to WebSocket clients, interfering with Luca Terminal (port 8089). Killing VT server resolved interference.

Implemented concurrent thread streaming support: streams continue running when switching threads, buffer accumulates in background, pulsing blue dot indicates active stream, content restores when switching back.
Sub-agent UI panel only shows agents spawned through LT proxy, not ones spawned through OpenClaw directly. This explains why some sub-agent activities don't show animations in sidebar.

Architected shared memory pool for Mnemos: single shared DB with visibility levels (PRIVATE, SHARED, PUBLIC), auto-publish hooks for significant events, conflict resolution. Created handoff documentation for Claude Code to implement.

Unified typography in the A2A comms pane to match the main chat's design tokens: name tags use --font-sans, 11px, uppercase, weight 500; body text uses --font-sans, 13px, weight 420. This ensures visual cohesion while keeping the pane distinct as a log-like overlay.

Separated the Intelligence Overview (personal/agent dashboard) from the geopolitical intelligence mockup, keeping only the Overview tab and removing external content sections (Twitter, Reddit, arXiv, markets, weather) for now. The Overview will be the core product while external signals can become a

Synchronous file scanning of 4,355+ session files every 3 seconds causes severe event loop blocking and app crashes. Need to use async methods or fs.watch on recent files only for sub-agent detection.
When rebuilding Luca Terminal Electron app, in-memory UI state (chat messages) is lost but session data persists on disk in JSONL files. The conversation remains intact in OpenClaw sessions.

Riley has three Anthropic auth profiles: 'anthropic:oauth' (original account), 'anthropic:manual' (currently active), and 'anthropic:temp' (second subscription account created March 30). The auth order determines which account is used first.

When debugging persistent connection/auth issues, a nuclear reset (deleting all profiles and starting fresh) can be more effective than incremental fixes when stale stream state accumulates from failed messages.
Riley experiences frequent rate limit throttling with Anthropic accounts, causing mid-response cutoffs and connection instability, especially during intensive development sessions.
Decided to kill the global agent dropdown in Luca Terminal in favor of thread-based agent context. Each thread carries its own agent ownership, and the sidebar shows all threads grouped by agent.
When modifying agent UI configurations, always add new agents alongside existing ones rather than replacing them. The pattern should be additive, not substitutive.
Removed auto-push canvas functionality entirely in favor of manual canvas pushes. The file watcher approach was too aggressive and opened internal memory files, creating noise.

When debugging canvas auto-opening issues, discovered that Vektor Terminal server (port 8088) was running in background and broadcasting archived conversation data to WebSocket clients, interfering with Luca Terminal.

Removed automatic canvas-push file watcher from Luca Terminal server because it was too aggressive (opening internal memory files) and unreliable. Replaced with manual canvas push: users can ask 'show that in canvas' and Vektor will push files via WebSocket.

When debugging canvas auto-open issues, discovered that Vektor Terminal server (port 8088) was running in background and broadcasting archived conversation data to WebSocket clients, interfering with Luca Terminal (port 8089). Killing VT server resolved the issue.

Agents need detailed codebase context and explicit 'do not' rules to avoid regressions. Codex replaced Anima with Jerry instead of adding alongside because it lacked understanding of the existing codebase patterns.

Removed the auto-push canvas file watcher system from Luca Terminal server.mjs because it was too aggressive (opening internal memory files) and unreliable. Replaced with manual canvas push: users can say 'show that in canvas' and Vektor will push files via WebSocket.

When debugging UI issues, check for multiple running instances or background processes that might be interfering. The canvas bug was caused by Vektor Terminal server broadcasting to the same WebSocket clients.
Removed auto-push file watcher from Luca Terminal canvas system in favor of manual WebSocket pushes on request.
Canvas system in Luca Terminal works via WebSocket push - when Vektor creates files, Riley can ask 'show that in canvas' and Vektor pushes them manually.
OpenClaw gateway doesn't expose tool calls in SSE streams - it runs full tool loops internally and only streams final text back, preventing stream interception approach for canvas detection.
When Riley asks to see something in canvas, Vektor should push it via WebSocket manually rather than relying on automatic detection.
Implemented a file watcher approach for auto-opening created files in canvas panel instead of stream interception because OpenClaw gateway doesn't expose tool calls in SSE stream.
Canvas system in Luca Terminal (canvas.js, WebSocket routing, REST API) works perfectly but lacked bridge between agent file writes and canvas opening.
File watcher safety rails: only watches during active streams, ignores dotfiles/node_modules/.git/.env/binaries, caps at 3 files per stream, skips files >500KB, degrades silently if fails.
When switching threads in Luca Terminal, clearMessages() destroys streaming message DOM elements but doesn't abort generation, causing orphaned streams.
Fix for thread switching bug: implement concurrent thread support with per-thread buffering, background stream completion, visual indicators for active threads, and restore-on-switch-back.
Stream infrastructure supports multiple threads with _activeStreams Map keyed by threadId, backend can handle concurrent streams.

When Codex reworked message routing to use `sendMultiAgentMessage` more broadly (even for single-target messages in group threads or non-default agents), it broke the thinking animation because the multi-agent path had no thinking logic wired up.

Fixed the thinking animation in the multi-agent message path by making four surgical edits: 1) Modified `startAgentMessage` to call `startThinking()`, 2) Updated `appendAgentChunk` to forward thinking text to `updateThinkingContent()` instead of discarding it, 3) Updated the `sendMultiAgentMessage`
Architectural changes that consolidate code paths (like routing all messages through `sendMultiAgentMessage`) can inadvertently break features that were only implemented in the old, now-unused paths.
The thinking animation consists of a visual block with murmur dots and a shimmer label that appears while the agent is processing, then settles before the response text is rendered.

The singleton pattern in thinking.js causes issues when multiple agents stream simultaneously, as they stomp on each other's thinking state. This was the root cause of the disappearing thinking animation in multi-agent mode.

Implemented a per-agent thinking block system for the multi-agent message path, moving away from the singleton global thinking element. This allows each agent to have independent thinking animations that don't interfere with each other.
The single-agent message path still uses the original singleton thinking system, while the multi-agent path now uses independent per-agent thinking blocks. Both systems coexist in the codebase.

When debugging UI streaming issues in multi-agent contexts, check for singleton patterns that assume single-user/single-agent operation. These often break when multiple entities need simultaneous state management.
Thinking animation disappears in multi-agent mode because thinking deltas are not rendered to DOM in the multi-agent path.
Fix thinking animation in multi-agent mode by ensuring thinking deltas call appendAgentChunk with isThinking=true and updateThinkingContent.
When implementing multi-agent features, ensure all UI components (like thinking animation) are properly integrated in both single-agent and multi-agent paths.
In connection.js, thinking deltas call appendAssistantChunk(text, true) which routes through messages.js → updateThinkingContent().
startThinking() is called via startAssistantMessage() in messages.js.
Transformed the Research view in Luca Terminal into a live Agent Dashboard with three-column layout: Agent Roster (left), Activity Feed (center), and Embedded Chat (right).
Implemented embedded chat using direct SSE fetch to gateway proxy instead of connection.sendMessage() to avoid side effects on main chat DOM.
Used polling (5s interval) for agent status updates instead of WebSocket implementation for simplicity.
Agent status visualization: working = amber pulse animation, done = green accent, error = red accent.
Added GET /api/subagents endpoint that proxies to OpenClaw gateway's sessions API and filters for sub-agent sessions.
When repurposing existing views, maintain the same export interface (initResearch, showResearch, hideResearch, resetResearch) to avoid breaking existing imports.
Embedded chat messages are saved to the thread via /api/threads/:id/messages endpoint for persistence.
Used better-sqlite3 for synchronous SQLite access in the Mnemos JS port to maintain schema compatibility with the Python version.
The Mnemos JS port reads/writes the same SQLite database as the Python version with identical schema, including 1,097 engrams, 5,798 connections, and 7 beliefs in the live vektor.db.
Stored JSON fields as TEXT in SQLite and parse on read/stringify on write to maintain compatibility with Python's JSON field handling.
LLM calls in the Mnemos JS port go through POST /v1/chat/completions to the LT server proxy at localhost:4001 by default.
Used ULID for engram IDs with 'engram_<ULID>' format to match the Python version's ID generation.

The Mnemos JS port implements all consolidation passes: decay, connection discovery, softening with lesson creation, belief review with asymmetric impact (0.07 support / 0.04 contradict), and reflection with graph-computed identity.
Retrieval uses 3-hop spreading activation with relation-type weights and reconsolidation that strengthens memories on retrieval (strength +0.05, stability +0.01).
When porting systems between Python and JavaScript, maintaining exact schema compatibility is critical for shared database access.
Polyphonic app (formerly Luca Terminal) has an onboarding system that writes configuration to ~/.polyphonic/ directory, not ~/.openclaw/.
Onboarding overlay is implemented as a sibling of #app (outside the app div, before Command Palette) rather than inside #app, with z-index: 1000 and position: fixed.
API key validation is done by calling OpenRouter's /models endpoint with the provided key.

OpenClaw configuration must follow exact format: auth.profiles uses 'mode': 'token' (never 'type'), model is { primary, fallbacks: [] } (never a plain string), gateway.mode is 'local', API key goes in .env file, agents.list has { 'id': 'main' }.
Onboarding completion triggers page reload to reinitialize the full app with new configuration present.
generateOpenClawConfig() logic from reference documentation is implemented directly in server endpoint rather than as separate import.
POST /api/setup/complete endpoint writes config JSON, .env file, workspace directory, and SOUL.md identity file.
Onboarding system must not import canvas.js or any view module to maintain separation of concerns.
Design tokens are sourced from public/css/tokens.css and never hardcoded in onboarding implementation.

Luca Terminal uses a specific architecture: Electron main process spawns server.mjs which serves static frontend on port 8089 and proxies to OpenClaw gateway on port 4141. Data flows from user input → connection.js → server proxy → OpenClaw → SSE stream back → messages.js renders.

Luca Terminal has 38 JavaScript modules in public/js/ organized into categories: core (connection, messages, threads), UI components (input, canvas, sidebar), views (fleet, command-center, system), and utilities (state, storage, utils).

Critical agent configuration in server.mjs: Vektor agent has ID 'main' (not 'vektor'), which is essential for routing. AGENTS config includes fields: id, name, model, systemPrompt, temperature, maxTokens, thinkingEnabled, color, avatar.

Multi-agent routing follows a specific chain: input.js creates pills → connection.js resolves target agent → server.mjs uses sessionKey-based routing with model rewriting for non-Vektor agents → OpenClaw gateway receives proper target.

Agent colors are implemented through a CSS chain: tokens.css defines --agent-{name}-color variables → messages.css/input.css use .agent-{name} classes → threads.js applies agent classes to messages → UI reflects assigned colors.

Thread switching follows a 14-step sequence: threads.js switchThread → clearMessages → fetch thread data → update URL → update sidebar → renderMessages → restore scroll position. Breaking this causes state corruption.

Server.mjs implements 70+ API endpoints across categories: thread management, message operations, agent interactions, system controls, and file operations. Key endpoints: POST /api/threads, POST /api/messages, GET /api/agents, POST /api/thinking.
Data storage uses three directories: threads/ (JSON thread files), artifacts/ (generated files), conversations/ (exported conversations). Thread files contain messages, metadata, and agent context.

Thinking animation uses a state machine: messages.js creates per-agent inline blocks with data-state attributes (thinking, streaming, done). CSS animations in thinking.css respond to state changes with pulsing effects.
Canvas panel integration: WebSocket open_canvas event → canvas.js handleCanvasEvent → render based on event type (init, update, clear). Canvas state is managed separately from main message flow.
View switching uses CSS classes: index.html defines major views (fleet-view, command-center-view, system-view) that are shown/hidden via .active class. Only one view can be active at a time.

The proxy in server.mjs performs critical auth injection and body rewriting for non-Vektor agents. It must preserve the original target agent while rewriting the model field to match OpenClaw expectations.

OpenClaw stores authentication tokens for Anthropic accounts in `~/.openclaw/agents/main/agent/auth-profiles.json`. Multiple profiles can exist simultaneously, identified by names like `anthropic:manual`, `anthropic:oauth`, and `anthropic:temp`.

The active Anthropic account is determined by the `auth.order.anthropic` array in the OpenClaw config. The gateway selects the first available profile in that list. The current order is `anthropic:temp` → `anthropic:manual` → `anthropic:oauth`.

To switch Vektor back to the original Anthropic account, the plan is to modify the auth order to prioritize `anthropic:manual` and `anthropic:oauth` over `anthropic:temp`, either via a CLI command or direct config edit.

When adding a new agent (like Jerry) to Luca Terminal, simply closing and reopening the Electron app window may not restart the underlying Express server if it's running as a separate process. The old server process must be killed first to release the port and allow the updated code to load.
OpenClaw's architecture allows different agents to be routed to different API providers and authentication profiles simultaneously within the same application.

Luca Terminal's Mission Control view uses a heat model (hot/warm/cool/cold/frozen) for threads, which is not just a UI metaphor but implements the same decay concept that drives Mnemos and Trace, applied to conversation flow.
Luca Terminal's Guardian block names creative phases (e.g., 'Evening Renaissance'), showing awareness that the timing of work matters as much as the content.

Luca Terminal's Intelligence view is structured as a hero brief with overnight agent activity, actionable decisions, and a belief confidence sidebar, representing a fully realized morning brief concept.
Polyphonic settings page will be built as a new view module (settings.js/settings.css) accessible from gear icon in sidebar, following existing view system patterns.

Luca Terminal's server reads all three Mnemos databases (Vektor, Riley, Luca) simultaneously and serves per-agent stats, modulators, and beliefs through a unified API.
Polyphonic config writing pattern: READ existing config first, deep-merge changes, write back. API keys go in .env ONLY, never in JSON config.
Luca Terminal's UI is structured like an IDE for cognition, with coherent information architecture across Mission Control, Intelligence, Memory Graph, Research, Financial, and Moltbook views.
Polyphonic model IDs use openrouter/ prefix format: `openrouter/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5`.

Polyphonic view system uses CSS classes to hide/show views (e.g., .view-intelligence-hidden) and each view module has its own hideView() function.

Discovered bundled Electron app server.mjs was ~1,100 lines behind working copy, causing canvas auto-push feature to not work until app rebuild.

Fixed Codex's changes that accidentally removed Anima agent from frontend while adding Jerry. Restored Anima in threads.js, messages.css, input.css alongside Jerry, added missing --jerry CSS variable.

Current Mnemos setup: each agent has isolated database (Vektor: ~/.mnemos/vektor.db, Anima: ~/.mnemos/anima.db). Cross-agent bridge cron creates shared summary file but no true shared memory pool. Schema supports owner_agent_id and visibility fields but unused.

Built and tested A2A (agent-to-agent) comms pane in Luca Terminal with full round-trip verification between Vektor and Luca. The pane expands the Electron window outward by 300px via IPC handlers and shows real-time agent messages with proper styling.
